message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Introduction</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Dynamic tools and methods to assess the ongoing transmission potential of COVID-19 in India are required. We aim to estimate time-dependent transmissibility of COVID-19 for India using a reproducible framework.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Methods</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    Daily COVID-19 case incidence time series for India and its states was obtained from
                    <jats:ext-link xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink" ext-link-type="uri" xlink:href="https://api.covid19india.org/">https://api.covid19india.org/</jats:ext-link>
                    and pre-processed. Bayesian approach was adopted to quantify transmissibility at a given location and time, as indicated by the instantaneous reproduction number (R
                    <jats:sub>eff</jats:sub>
                    ). Analysis was carried out in R version 4.0.2 using
                    <jats:italic>“EpiEstim_2.2-3”</jats:italic>
                    package. Serial interval distribution was estimated using “uncertain_si” algorithm with inputs of mean, standard deviation, minimum and maximum of mean serial interval as 5.1, 1.2, 3.9 and 7.5 days respectively; and mean, standard deviation, minimum, and maximum of standard deviations of serial interval as 3.7, 0.9, 2.3, and 4.7 respectively with 100 simulations and moving average of seven days.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    A total of 9,07,544 cumulative incident cases till July 13
                    <jats:sup>th</jats:sup>
                    , 2020 were analysed. Daily COVID-19 incidence in the country was seen on the rise; however, transmissibility showed a decline from the initial phases of COVID-19 pandemic in India. The maximum R
                    <jats:sub>eff</jats:sub>
                    reached at the national level during the study period was 2.57 (sliding week ending April 4
                    <jats:sup>th</jats:sup>
                    , 2020). R
                    <jats:sub>eff</jats:sub>
                    on July 13
                    <jats:sup>th</jats:sup>
                    , 2020 for India was 1.16 with a range from 0.59 to 2.98 across various states/UTs.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusion</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    R
                    <jats:sub>eff</jats:sub>
                    provides critical feedback for assessment of transmissibility of COVID-19 and thus is a potential dynamic decision support tool for on-ground public health decision making.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title: Time-dependent dynamic transmission potential and instantaneous reproduction number of COVID-19 pandemic in India

reference.unstructured: WHO. [Internet]. Coronavirus Disease (COVID-19) Dashboard. World Health Organisation. Available from: https://covid19.who.int [cited July 14, 2020].

Anderson RM , May RM . Infectious diseases of humans: dynamics and control, Reprinted. Oxford: Oxford Univ. Press, 2010.
COVID19-India API. [ Internet] Available on https://api.covid19india.org/csv/ (accessed July 14, 2020).

Patrikar S , Kotwal A , Bhatti V , et al. Incubation Period and Reproduction Number for novel coronavirus (COVID-19) infections in India. Infectious Diseases (except HIV/AIDS), 2020 DOI:10.1101/2020.06.27.20141424.
MHA, India. [Internet]. Circulars for Covid-19. Ministry of Home Affairs, Government of India. Available from: https://www.mha.gov.in/notifications/circulars-covid-19 [cited July 14, 2020].
